prompt:
ܦܪܩܐ ܐ

1:1 ܫܡܰܥ ܡܶܠܬ݂ܳܐ ܗܳܕ݂ܶܐ ܘܣܺܝܡ ܒܠܶܒܳܟ݂ ܬܠܳܬ݂ ܥܶܠ̈ܠܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܡܶܛܽܠܳܬ݂ܗܶܝܢ ܟܶܣܦܳܐ ܕܰܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܠܳܐ ܢܶܬ݂ܺܝܗܶܒ ܬܽܘܒ ܠܒ̈ܳܬܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ܀
(Hear this word and set in your heart three reasons on account of which the silver of the cities should not be given again to the houses of books of the people.)

1:2 ܩܰܕ݂ܡܳܝܬ݂ ܗܳܐ ܒܝܰܘ̈ܡܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܗܳܠܶܝܢ ܟܽܠ ܐ̱ܢܳܫ ܐܺܝܬ݂ ܠܶܗ ܡܰܥܒܪܳܢܽܘܬ݂ܳܐ ܥܰܠ ܝܺܕ݂ܰܥܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܥܰܠ ܟܬ݂ܳܒ̈ܶܐ ܡܶܢ ܒܰܝܬ݂ܶܗ ܒܝܰܕ݂ ܡܳܐ̈ܢܶܐ ܕܰܚܫܽܘ̈ܒܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܫܽܘ̈ܩܶܐ ܕܰܙܒ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܒ݂ܰܐܩܠܺܝ̈ܡܶܐ ܕܡܶܨ̈ܥܳܝܶܐ ܀ ܘܡܶܟܺܝܠ ܒ̈ܳܬ݂ܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ ܗܘܰܘ ܐܰܝܟ ܐ̱ܢܳܫ ܕܡܰܘܫܶܛ ܡ̈ܰـܝܳܐ ܒܡܰܕ݂ܒܪܳܐ ܟܰܕ݂ ܢܰܗܪ̈ܶܐ ܣܰܓ̈ܺܝ̈ܐܶܐ ܐܶܫܬ݂ܟ̈ܰܚܘ ܒܟ݂ܽܠ ܐܰܬ݂ܪ ܀ ܡܶܛܽܠ ܕܣܽܘܢܩܳܢܳܐ ܕܡܶܢ ܩܕ݂ܺܝܡ ܐܺܝܬ݂ ܗ̱ܘܳܐ ܗܳܫܳܐ ܠܳܐ ܐܺܝܬ݂ ܬܽܘܒ ܀
(First: behold in these days every man has passage unto knowledge and unto books from his house by means of instruments of reckoning and marketplaces of commerce that are in the digital realms. And therefore the houses of books of the people have become like a man who stretches out water in the wilderness when many rivers have been found in every place. For the need that was from of old, now it is not anymore.)

1:3 ܬܶܢܝܳܢܺܝܬ݂ ܗܳܐ ܒ̈ܳܬ݂ܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ ܢܳܣܒ̈ܺܝܢ ܡܶܢ ܟܶܣܦܳܐ ܕܡܰܕ̈ܰܐܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܟ݂ܽܠ ܐ̱ܢܳܫ ܡܶܢ ܥܰܡܳܐ ܐܳܦ ܡܶܢ ܐܰܝܠܶܝܢ ܕܠܳܐ ܡܶܡܬ݂ܽܘܡ ܥܳܐܠܺܝܢ ܒܬ݂ܰܪ̈ܥܰܝܗܽܘܢ ܘܠܳܐ ܩܳܪܶܝܢ ܟܬ݂ܳܒ̈ܰܝܗܽܘܢ ܀ ܘܟ݂ܶܣܦܳܐ ܗܳܢܳܐ ܕܡܶܬ݂ܢܣܶܒ ܡܶܢ ܐ̱ܢ̈ܳܫܳܐ ܒܩܰܛܺܝܪܳܐ ܡܶܫܟ̈ܚܳܢ ܗ̱ܘ̈ܰܝ ܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܠܡܶܦܠܚ ܒܶܗ ܥܰܠ ܐܽܘܪ̈ܚܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܥܰܠ ܒܶܛܢ̈ܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܰܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܘܥܰܠ ܚܽܘ̈ܠܡܳܢܶܐ ܕܓ݂ܰܘ̈ܳܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ ܀ ܘܟ݂ܰܕ ܟ̈ܢܳـܘܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܕܨܶ̈ܒ̈ـܘܳܬ݂ܳܐ ܩܰܫ̈ـܝܳـܬ݂ܳܐ ܐܺܝ̈ـܬ݂ ܒ̈ـܟ̈ـܽـܠ ܡܕ݂ܺܝ̈ـܢ̈ـܳـܬ݂ܳܐ ܠܳܐ ܝܳـܐ̱ ܗ̱ـܽـܘ ܕܢܶـ̈ـܬ̈ـܺـܝ̈ـܗ̈ـܶـ̈ـ̈ ܟ̈ـ̈ـ ܕ̈ ܣ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈

ܝ ̈ ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈ ̈ ̈ ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈  ̈
response:
# ܦܪܩܐ ܐ — Analysis and Completion

This is a fascinating text written in **Classical Syriac** (ܣܘܪܝܝܐ), composed in the style of biblical prophetic literature, but addressing a thoroughly **modern political argument**: that public libraries should no longer receive municipal funding.

The text appears to have become corrupted at the end of verse 1:3. Let me summarize what is present and reconstruct the argument:

---

## Summary of the Text

**Verse 1:1** — The thesis: "Hear this word and set it in your heart — three reasons why the silver of the cities should no longer be given to the houses of books of the people (i.e., public libraries)."

**Verse 1:2** — **First reason:** In the digital age, everyone has access to knowledge and books from home through computers ("instruments of reckoning") and online marketplaces ("marketplaces of commerce in the digital realms"). Public libraries have become like someone offering water in the desert when rivers are found everywhere — the original need no longer exists.

**Verse 1:3** — **Second reason:** Public libraries take tax money from everyone, including those who never enter their doors or read their books. This money, taken by compulsion, could instead be spent on roads, city infrastructure, and public health. When cities face many pressing needs, it is not fitting that silver be given to...

Here the text **breaks down into garbled syame (plural marks)** and becomes unreadable — likely a manuscript corruption or encoding failure.

---

## The Missing Third Reason

The text promises **three reasons** (ܬܠܳܬ݂ ܥܶܠ̈ܠܳܬ݂ܳܐ) but only two are given before the corruption. A plausible **third reason** in keeping with the rhetorical style might concern the argument that private enterprise or charitable organizations could fulfill the same role more efficiently — a common libertarian argument against publicly funded libraries.

---

## Observations on the Language

The Syriac is remarkably well-formed Classical/Literary Syriac with full vocalization (vowel diacritics). The author skillfully coins neologisms for modern concepts:

| Syriac Term | Literal Meaning | Modern Concept |
|---|---|---|
| ܒ̈ܳܬ݂ܶܐ ܕܣܶܦܪ̈ܶܐ ܕܥܰܡܳܐ | "houses of books of the people" | public libraries |
| ܡܳܐ̈ܢܶܐ ܕܰܚܫܽܘ̈ܒܳܬ݂ܳܐ | "instruments of reckonings" | computers |
| ܫܽܘ̈ܩܶܐ ܕܰܙܒ݂ܺܝ̈ܢܳܬ݂ܳܐ | "marketplaces of commerce" | online stores (e.g., Amazon) |
| ܐܰܩܠܺܝ̈ܡܶܐ ܕܡܶܨ̈ܥܳܝܶܐ | "digital/middle realms" | the internet/digital space |
| ܟܶܣܦܳܐ ܕܡܰܕ̈ܰܐܬ݂ܳܐ | "silver of taxes" | tax revenue |
| ܡܰܥܒܪܳܢܽܘܬ݂ܳܐ | "passage/crossing" | access |

The prophetic register — with imperatives like **ܫܡܰܥ** ("Hear!") and demonstrative constructions echoing Amos, Isaiah, and the Peshitta — creates an ironic juxtaposition between ancient sacred rhetoric and a modern fiscal-conservative policy argument.

Would you like me to attempt a reconstruction of the corrupted ending and the missing third reason in the same Syriac style?
